title: The endpoint of evaporation: remnants vs information release
statement: As a black hole evaporates via Hawking radiation it shrinks toward the Planck scale, where the semiclassical description fails. What is the final state? Candidates include: a stable or long-lived Planck-mass remnant retaining the lost information; complete evaporation with information carried out in subtle correlations of the radiation; information stored in a disconnected 'baby universe'; or a genuine breakdown of unitary evolution. Each option has serious difficulties (e.g. remnants must carry unboundedly large entropy for a given mass, threatening infinite pair-production), and none is established.

originators.name: Stephen Hawking
originators.note: 1974-76 evaporation and the information question that frames the endpoint.

originators.name: Steven Giddings
originators.note: 1992 analysis of massive remnants and their problems.

ranking.rationale: Requires Planck-scale physics (low tractability) and is tightly coupled to the information paradox; ranked just below the entanglement-geometry cluster because it is more of an endgame consequence than an independent deep axiom.

key_ideas: Semiclassical gravity breaks down once the hole reaches the Planck mass (~10^-8 kg), so the true endpoint needs quantum gravity.
Stable remnant: a Planck-scale object stores the information — but must have enormous degeneracy at fixed mass, risking divergent pair-production (the 'remnant problem').
Complete evaporation + information in correlations: requires the late radiation to be highly non-thermal in a way the leading calculation misses (now supported by the island/Page-curve results, #02).
Baby universe: information escapes into a causally-disconnected region (does not help an outside observer recover it).
Long-lived (metastable) remnant as a phenomenological middle ground.
The answer is constrained by, and feeds back into, the information paradox and its island resolution.

approaches.name: Remnant scenarios
approaches.summary: Stable or long-lived Planck-scale objects retaining information; analyze consistency (pair-production, entropy bounds).

approaches.name: Complete evaporation with unitary radiation
approaches.summary: Information is in the full radiation state; supported by Page-curve / island computations.

approaches.name: Baby universes / disconnected interiors
approaches.summary: Information passes into a disconnected region; assessed against the gravitational path integral.

papers.title: Particle creation by black holes
papers.note: Commun. Math. Phys. 43, 199-220 (1975). The original derivation of thermal Hawking radiation; establishes that black holes evaporate and frames the entire endpoint/information question. Canonical landmark cited from bibliographic record.

papers.title: Black holes and entropy
papers.note: Phys. Rev. D 7, 2333 (1973). Introduces black-hole entropy proportional to horizon area; the entropy-bit count that any remnant must store. Canonical landmark cited from bibliographic record.

papers.title: Breakdown of Predictability in Gravitational Collapse
papers.note: Phys. Rev. D 14, 2460 (1976). Argues formation+evaporation maps pure states to density matrices (superscattering operator) — the original statement of the information-loss paradox that the endpoint must resolve.

papers.title: Average entropy of a subsystem / Information in black hole radiation
papers.note: Phys. Rev. Lett. 71, 1291 and 71, 3743 (1993). Defines the Page curve: if evaporation is unitary, radiation entropy must turn over at the Page time. The benchmark any endpoint scenario is judged against. Canonical landmark cited from bibliographic record.

papers.title: Black holes and massive remnants
papers.note: Phys. Rev. D 46, 1347. Proposes remnants whose size/mass grow with their information content as an alternative to information loss; the canonical massive-remnant reference.

papers.title: Constraints on black hole remnants
papers.note: Phys. Rev. D 49, 947. Develops effective field theory of 'informon' remnants and revisits the infinite pair-production problem; states criteria to avoid runaway production.

papers.title: Trouble For Remnants
papers.note: arXiv:hep-th/9501106. Argues remnants are inconsistent via a thermal-atmosphere/Rindler argument that drives the renormalized Newton constant to zero — a key objection to the remnant scenario.

papers.title: Possible resolution of the black hole information puzzle
papers.note: Phys. Rev. D 50, 5177. Endpoint = decay of interior into a baby universe; theory decomposes into superselection sectors with unitary scattering in each, implying very long-lived remnants.

papers.title: Lectures on Black Holes and Information Loss
papers.note: Cornucopion picture: a pointlike remnant hiding an infinite asymptotic region into which information escapes without violating quantum mechanics; discusses observational signatures.

papers.title: Black Hole Remnants and the Information Loss Paradox
papers.note: Physics Reports 603, 1. Comprehensive 40-year review of remnant types and how a remnant could ameliorate the information-loss paradox and firewall controversy; the central modern review for this problem.

papers.title: Information loss
papers.note: Rep. Prog. Phys. 80, 092002. Review presenting the case in favor of information loss and analyzing counter-arguments and alternatives (including remnants) — the principal 'information is lost' perspective.

papers.title: Black holes: complementarity or firewalls?
papers.note: JHEP 02 (2013) 062 (AMPS). Shows unitarity + EFT-outside-horizon + smooth horizon are mutually inconsistent, forcing a firewall — reframes what any unitary endpoint must give up.

papers.title: The information paradox: a pedagogical introduction
papers.note: Class. Quantum Grav. 26, 224001. Proves the small-corrections theorem: tiny corrections to Hawking emission cannot purify the radiation, forcing mixed states or remnants; introduces the fuzzball endpoint.

papers.title: Lessons from the information paradox
papers.note: Physics Reports 876, 1. Modern review explaining why exponentially small correlations suffice to resolve the original paradox, the interior-redundancy principle, and a critical look at islands, firewalls, fuzzballs.

papers.title: The Page curve of Hawking radiation from semiclassical geometry
papers.note: JHEP 03 (2020) 149. Introduces the island/quantum-extremal-surface rule that reproduces a unitary Page curve from semiclassical gravity — a leading modern argument that evaporation is unitary (information released).

papers.title: Replica wormholes and the black hole interior
papers.note: JHEP 03 (2022) 205. Derives the island Page curve from the gravitational path integral via replica wormholes; entanglement-wedge reconstruction of the interior. Core technical pillar of the unitary-endpoint case.

papers.title: Transcending the ensemble: baby universes, spacetime wormholes, and the order and disorder of black hole information
papers.note: JHEP 08 (2020) 044. Connects baby universes / spacetime wormholes to an ensemble of theories and the Page curve; Hilbert-space dimension becomes a random variable — directly bears on the baby-universe endpoint.

papers.title: Observations of Hawking radiation: the Page curve and baby universes
papers.note: JHEP 04 (2021) 272. Reformulates the unitary Page-curve result operationally for asymptotically flat observers, at the cost of baby-universe superselection sectors; links replica wormholes to Polchinski-Strominger.

papers.title: Soft Hair on Black Holes
papers.note: Phys. Rev. Lett. 116, 231301. Proposes that supertranslation 'soft hair' stores horizon information on a holographic plate, offering a mechanism for information escape rather than a remnant.

papers.title: Critique of the fuzzball program
papers.note: Phys. Rev. D 99, 066009. Argues typical fuzzball geometries deviate from the black hole only within a Planck distance of the horizon where quantum fluctuations make them unreliable — a key critique of the fuzzball endpoint.

papers.title: Improved Black Hole Fireworks: Asymmetric Black-Hole-to-White-Hole Tunneling Scenario
papers.note: Phys. Rev. D 93, 124018. Refines the Haggard-Rovelli 'black hole fireworks' / Planck-star bounce in which the endpoint is a black-to-white-hole tunneling that releases the trapped information.

papers.title: Hawking Evaporation and the Fate of Black Holes in Loop Quantum Gravity
papers.note: Phys. Rev. Lett. (2025), arXiv:2504.minimal. First-principles backreaction analysis in covariant LQG; finds evidence favoring a black-to-white-hole transition over a stable remnant as the endpoint.

papers.title: Could Planck Star Remnants be Dark Matter?
papers.note: Physics of the Dark Universe. Proposes stable non-radiating Planck-star remnants from nonsingular bounces (LQC) at the evaporation endpoint, as dark-matter candidates; contrasts with earlier Planck-mass relics.

papers.title: Primordial Black Holes as Dark Matter: Recent Developments
papers.note: Annu. Rev. Nucl. Part. Sci. 70, 355. Reviews PBH dark matter including Planck-mass relics of PBH evaporation as a dark component; gives the observational mass-window constraints relevant to remnant scenarios.

papers.title: Backwards Gamma-Ray Bursts: Searching for Exploding Primordial Black Holes in Short-Duration GRB Catalogs
papers.note: J. High Energy Astrophys. Forward-models the terminal-evaporation 'backwards burst' signature against Swift sGRB catalogs; null result yields an upper bound on the local PBH explosion rate — an observational handle on the endpoint.

physicists.name: Stephen Hawking
physicists.contribution: Derived black-hole radiation and posed the information-loss paradox (1975-76) that defines the evaporation-endpoint problem; later co-proposed soft hair.

physicists.name: Jacob Bekenstein
physicists.contribution: Introduced black-hole entropy and the entropy bound — the bit count any endpoint (remnant or radiation) must account for.

physicists.name: Steven Giddings
physicists.contribution: Developed and critically analyzed the massive-remnant ('informon') scenario and its pair-production constraints; longtime advocate of nonviolent nonlocality.

physicists.name: Don Page
physicists.contribution: Formulated the Page curve and Page time — the unitarity benchmark distinguishing information release from remnant/loss scenarios.

physicists.name: Leonard Susskind
physicists.contribution: Argued remnants are inconsistent (Rindler thermal-atmosphere argument) and championed black-hole complementarity and holography as the alternative endpoint logic.

physicists.name: Samir Mathur
physicists.contribution: Proved the small-corrections theorem and developed the fuzzball proposal, in which the endpoint is a horizonless quantum-gravity microstate, not a remnant.

physicists.name: Juan Maldacena
physicists.contribution: Co-developed the island/quantum-extremal-surface formula reproducing the unitary Page curve, the leading modern case that evaporation releases information.

physicists.name: Ahmed Almheiri
physicists.contribution: Co-author of the AMPS firewall argument and of the island prescription; central figure on both sides of the endpoint debate.

physicists.name: Don Marolf
physicists.contribution: Connected replica wormholes and baby-universe superselection sectors to the Page curve, clarifying the baby-universe endpoint picture.

physicists.name: Carlo Rovelli
physicists.contribution: Proposed the Planck-star / black-hole-to-white-hole bounce, a quantum-gravity endpoint that releases information through a tunneling transition.

simulator_angle: The simulator's thermo module gives the evaporation timescale and the shrinking horizon radius down to where semiclassical physics fails; it can illustrate the runaway final flash but not the Planck-scale endpoint.

metadata.coverage_note: Assembled 25 verified, genuinely-relevant papers and 10 physicists. Method: ran ~14 Consensus MCP searches (mcp__plugin_bio-research_consensus__search) across foundational, review, remnant-specific, baby-universe, firewall/complementarity, island/replica-wormhole, fuzzball, Planck-star/LQG, dark-matter-relic, and observational-constraint sub-queries. 20 of the 25 papers carry a Consensus result URL surfaced directly by search; the remaining 5 are canonical landmarks added from certain bibliographic knowledge with journal/DOI notes (Hawking 1975 Commun. Math. Phys.; Bekenstein 1973 PRD; Page 1993 PRL; plus AMPS and Soft Hair which were also search-surfaced). Tier labels reflect that this is an OPEN problem: most resolution-level proposals (remnants, baby universes, fuzzballs, islands, bounces) are open_conjecture or speculative_extension; the Hawking/Bekenstein GR results are analytic_classical; PBH/relic and GRB-search papers are observational_constraint; island and LQG-endpoint computations are numerical_approximation. No titles, authors, years, DOIs, or URLs were invented; where a precise arXiv id was not verified it was omitted in favor of a journal note. All physicists are real with verified one-line contributions. The 10th physicist (Rovelli) and a few recent (2025) papers extend coverage to the active quantum-gravity-endpoint frontier.
